perturbation.original_text: Confidential Information shall not include information that: a. is or becomes known publicly through no fault of the Recipient; or b. is learned by the Recipient from a third party not under an obligation of confi- dence to the Discloser and who has legitimate and legal possession of the informa- tion; or c. is already known to the Recipient before receipt from the Discloser as can be proven by written records; or d. is independently developed by employees or consultants of the Recipient who did not have access to any Confidential Information received; e. is free of confidentiality restrictions by written agreement of the Discloser.
perturbation.changed_text: Confidential Information shall not include information that: a. is or becomes known publicly through no fault of the Recipient; or b. is learned by the Recipient from a third party not under an obligation of confi- dence to the Discloser and who has legitimate and legal possession of the informa- tion; or c. is already known to the Recipient before receipt from the Discloser; or d. is independently developed by employees or consultants of the Recipient; e. is free of confidentiality restrictions by written agreement of the Discloser.
perturbation.explanation: Removed the requirement for written records to prove prior knowledge, conflicting with standard evidentiary requirements.
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the phrase 'as can be proven by written records,' the modified text contradicts established legal standards for admissible evidence. FRE 803(b)(3) outlines exceptions to the hearsay rule, but generally requires some form of reliable evidence to support claims, especially in commercial disputes. This modification attempts to bypass that requirement, potentially making it harder to enforce the 'prior knowledge' exception in court, as a party could claim prior knowledge without any documentary proof.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 803. Exceptions to the Rule Against Hearsay | Federal Rules of Evidence | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 803. Exceptions to the Rule Against Hearsay The following are not excluded by the rule against hearsay, regardless of whether the declarant is available as a witness: (1) Present Sense Impression . A statement describing or explaining an event or condition, made while or immediately after the declarant perceived it. (2) Excited Utterance . A statement relating to a startling event or condition, made while the declarant was under the stress of excitement that it caused. (3) Then-Existing Mental, Emotional, or Physical Condition. A statement of the declarant’s then-existing state of mind (such as motive, intent, or plan) or emotional, sensory, or physical condition (such as mental feeling, pain, or bodily health), but not including a statement of memory or belief to prove the fact remembered or believed unless it relates to the validity or terms of the declarant’s will. (4) Statement Made for Medical Diagnosis or Treatment . A statement that: (A) is made for — and is reasonably pertinent to — medical diagnosis or treatment; and (B) describes medical history; past or present symptoms or sensations; their inception; or their general cause. (5) Recorded Recollection. A record that: (A) is on a matter the witness once knew about but now cannot recall well enough to testify fully and accurately; (B) was made or adopted by the witness when the matter was fresh in the witness’s memory; and (C) accurately reflects the witness’s knowledge. If admitted, the record may be read into evidence but may be received as an exhibit only if offered by an adverse party. (6) Records of a Regularly Conducted Activity . A record of an act, event, condition, opinion, or diagnosis if: (A) the record was made at or near the time by — or from information transmitted by — someone with knowledge; (B) the record was kept in the course

perturbation.justification: The original text requires 'written records' to prove prior knowledge, providing a clear standard for evidence. The changed text removes this requirement, potentially allowing claims of prior knowledge without any documentation. This contradicts the need for reliable evidence in legal disputes, as the Law Explanation states. While Snippet 1 generally outlines exceptions to hearsay, the omission of 'written records' weakens the enforceability of the confidentiality agreement by removing a specific evidentiary requirement.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party warrants that it has the right to disclose all of the Confidential Information it discloses, but does not otherwise make any representation as to the ac-curacy, suitability, or completeness of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party warrants that it has the right to disclose all of the Confidential Information it discloses, and also warrants the accuracy, suitability, and completeness of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The added warranty creates a conflict by expanding the scope of liability related to the disclosed information.
perturbation.law_explanation: By warranting the accuracy, suitability, and completeness of confidential information, the modified text potentially creates an express warranty under UCC § 2-313, even if the information doesn't qualify as 'goods.' This could expose the Discloser to liability for breach of warranty if the information is later found to be inaccurate or unsuitable, even if disclosed in good faith. The original text specifically disclaims these warranties, aligning with standard practice in NDA agreements.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-313. Express Warranties by Affirmation, Promise, Description, Sample. (1) Express warranties by the seller are created as follows: (a) Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise. (b) Any description of the goods which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the description. (c) Any sample or model which is made part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the whole of the goods shall conform to the sample or model. (2) It is not necessary to the creation of an express warranty that the seller use formal words such as "warrant" or "guarantee" or that he have a specific intention to make a warranty, but an affirmation merely of the value of the goods or a statement purporting to be merely the seller's opinion or commendation of the goods does not create a warranty. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces a warranty regarding the accuracy, suitability, and completeness of confidential information, which the original text specifically disclaims. This creates a legal contradiction because, as the Law Explanation indicates, this could be interpreted as an express warranty under UCC § 2-313, potentially exposing the disclosing party to liability. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing UCC § 2-313, states that 'Any affirmation of fact or promise made by the seller to the buyer which relates to the goods and becomes part of the basis of the bargain creates an express warranty that the goods shall conform to the affirmation or promise.' While confidential information may not be 'goods' in the traditional sense, the LLM-provided Law Explanation notes that this section of the UCC could still apply, which would contradict the original agreement that specifically disclaims these warranties. This aligns with the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, where the terminology is being misaligned to create a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party agrees that the wrongful disclosure of Confidential In- Formation may cause irreparable injury that is inadequately compensable by monetary damages. As such, either Party may seek injunctive relief in any court of competent jurisdiction for the breach and/or the threatened breach of this Agreement in addition to any other remedies it may seek at law or equity, and the Recipient will not raise the defense of an adequate remedy at law.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party agrees that the wrongful disclosure of Confidential In- Formation may cause injury. As such, either Party may seek relief in any court for the breach and/or the threatened breach of this Agreement in addition to any other remedies it may seek at law or equity, and the Recipient may raise the defense of an adequate remedy at law.
perturbation.explanation: Removed the clause about 'irreparable injury' and 'inadequately compensable by monetary damages', and allowing Recipient to raise the defense of an adequate remedy at law, making injunctive relief less certain.
perturbation.law_explanation: Removing the acknowledgment of 'irreparable injury' and allowing the Recipient to raise the defense of 'adequate remedy at law' undermines the basis for seeking injunctive relief. FRCP 65 governs the issuance of injunctions, and courts typically require a showing of irreparable harm for injunctive relief to be granted. By creating a situation where monetary damages might be deemed sufficient, the modified text makes it more difficult for the Discloser to obtain an injunction, potentially weakening the protection of confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 65. Injunctions and Restraining Orders (a) Preliminary Injunction. (1) Notice. The court may issue a preliminary injunction only on notice to the adverse party. (2) Consolidating the Hearing with the Trial on the Merits. Before or after beginning the hearing on a motion for a preliminary injunction, the court may advance the trial on the merits and consolidate it with the hearing. Even when consolidation is not ordered, evidence that is received on the motion and that would be admissible at trial becomes part of the trial record and need not be repeated at trial. But the court must preserve any party's right to a jury trial. (b) Temporary Restraining Order. (1) Issuing Without Notice. The court may issue a temporary restraining order without written or oral notice to the adverse party or its attorney only if: (A) specific facts in an affidavit or a verified complaint clearly show that immediate and irreparable injury, loss, or damage will result to the movant before the adverse party can be heard in opposition; and (B) the movant's attorney certifies in writing any efforts made to give notice and the reasons why it should not be required. (2) Contents; Expiration. Every temporary restraining order issued without notice must state the date and hour it was issued; describe the injury and state why it is irreparable; state why the order was issued without notice; and be promptly filed in the clerk's office and entered in the record. The order expires at the time after entry—not to exceed 14 days—that the court sets, unless before that time the court, for good cause, extends it for a like period or the adverse party consents to a longer extension. The reasons for an extension must be entered in the record. (3) Expediting the Preliminary-Injunction Hearing. If the order is issued without notice, the moti
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. Read the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure (PDF) Related Downloads Federal Rules of Civil Procedure 431.61 KB Return to top Your browser does not support Javascript. Your browser does not support Javascript.
perturbation.justification: The changed text contradicts established legal principles regarding injunctive relief, specifically by removing the acknowledgment of 'irreparable injury' and allowing the defense of 'adequate remedy at law'. This undermines the ability to obtain an injunction, as FRCP 65, mentioned in 'Scraped Law Snippet 1', implies the necessity of demonstrating 'immediate and irreparable injury' for injunctive relief, especially in the context of temporary restraining orders issued without notice. By potentially allowing monetary damages to be deemed sufficient, the modified text weakens the protection of confidential information and contradicts the basis for seeking injunctive relief, a standard remedy in such cases.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of [state] without regard to any conflict of law principles.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be construed in accordance with the laws of the State of [state], with regard to any conflict of law principles.
perturbation.explanation: By including conflict of law principles, the governing law becomes uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: By stating that the agreement shall be construed 'with regard to any conflict of law principles', the modification directly invites a conflict of laws analysis. Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 allows parties to choose governing law but subjects it to limitations, particularly if the chosen state has no substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction, or if applying the chosen law would violate a fundamental policy of a state with a materially greater interest. The original clause sought to avoid this analysis and have [state] law apply directly. This modification makes the application of law uncertain and potentially unenforceable depending on a court's conflict of laws determination, contradicting the intent of a clear choice-of-law provision.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: About | HeinOnline Law Journal Library | HeinOnline Law Journal Library | HeinOnline Please click here if you are not redirected within a few seconds. What Is HeinOnline? HeinOnline is a subscription-based resource containing thousands of academic and legal journals from inception; complete coverage of government documents such as U.S. Statutes at Large, U.S. Code, Federal Register, Code of Federal Regulations, U.S. Reports, and much more. Documents are image-based, fully searchable PDFs with the authority of print combined with the accessibility of a user-friendly and powerful database. For more information, request a quote or trial for your organization below. profiles profiles most
perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a legal contradiction by altering the choice-of-law provision. The original text specifies that the agreement should be construed in accordance with the laws of [state] without regard to any conflict of law principles, ensuring the direct application of [state] law. The changed text, however, states that the agreement shall be construed with regard to any conflict of law principles. This modification introduces uncertainty, as it opens the door to a conflict of laws analysis, potentially leading to the application of laws other than those of [state]. The Law Explanation supports this by stating that this change contradicts the intent of a clear choice-of-law provision, potentially making the application of law uncertain. While the provided scraped snippets are not directly relevant, the Law Explanation provides a sufficient basis for identifying the contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the written request of the Discloser, the Recipient shall either destroy or return to the Discloser any and all Confidential Information in the Recipient’s possession, except for one copy, which copy may be retained for archival purposes in a secure file.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the request of the Discloser, the Recipient shall either destroy or return to the Discloser any and all Confidential Information in the Recipient’s possession, and the Recipient is allowed to retain copies of that information for internal use.
perturbation.explanation: Removes the clause limiting archival copies to one and specifies that copies may be retained for internal use, increasing the risk of disclosure.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, 18 U.S. Code § 1836
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing the Recipient to retain copies of the confidential information 'for internal use' without limitations, this change increases the risk of misappropriation, even if unintentional. The Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA) protects trade secrets, and unauthorized retention or disclosure can lead to liability. The original clause limited retention to a single archival copy kept securely, minimizing this risk. This modification creates a broader exception that could be interpreted as encouraging or permitting actions that violate the DTSA, particularly if the internal use leads to economic harm for the Discloser.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 18 U.S. Code § 1836 - Civil proceedings U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.— (1) In general.— An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.— (A) In general.— (i) Application.— Based on an affidavit or verified complaint satisfying the requirements of this paragraph, the court may, upon ex parte application but only in extraordinary circumstances, issue an order providing for the seizure of property necessary to prevent the propagation or dissemination of the trade secret that is the subject of the action. (ii) Requirements for issuing order.— The court may not grant an application under clause (i) unless the court finds that it clearly appears from specific facts that— (I) an order issued pursuant to Rule 65 of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure or another form of equitable relief would be inadequate to achieve the purpose of this paragraph because the party to which the order would be issued would evade, avoid, or otherwise not comply with such an order; (II) an immediate and irreparable injury will occur if such seizure is not ordered; (III) the harm to the applicant of denying the application outweighs the harm to the legitimate interests of the person against whom seizure would be ordered of granting the application and substantially outweighs the harm to any third parties who may be harmed by such seizure; (IV) the applicant is likely to succeed in showing that— (aa) the information is a trade secret ; and (bb) the person against whom seizure would be ordered— (AA
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 404 | Berkman Klein Center Skip to the main content Page Cannot Be Found Brave traveler, your journey on our site has brought you to an abrupt dead end. Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text allows the Recipient to retain copies of confidential information 'for internal use' without any limitation which increases the risk of misappropriation. This contradicts the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), which protects trade secrets. The original text limited this retention to a single archival copy kept securely, but the modified language creates a broader exception that could lead to economic harm for the Discloser. Scraped Law Snippet 1 references 18 U.S. Code § 1836, which allows the owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated to bring a civil action. By allowing copies for internal use the trade secret could be misappropriated.
